summary: Gruber highlights how spectacularly bad Siri is at answering simple factual questions, building on Paul Kafasis's exhaustive test showing Siri correctly identified only 34% of Super Bowl winners. He then devises his own test — asking about the 2004 North Dakota high school boys' basketball championship — and finds that ChatGPT, Kagi, and DuckDuckGo all answer correctly while Siri with Apple Intelligence gives confidently wrong answers that change each time. Most damningly, old Siri without Apple Intelligence performed better by simply providing useful web links rather than fabricating wrong answers. Gruber concludes that Apple Intelligence has made Siri a regression, and that OpenAI's brand is being harmed by the partnership since ChatGPT gets blamed for Siri's mangled answers despite answering perfectly when used directly.
key_points: Siri correctly identified only 34% of Super Bowl winners when tested systematically, including crediting the Philadelphia Eagles with 33 wins they never earned

Apple Intelligence Siri is a regression from old Siri: the old version at least provided useful web links when it didn't know an answer, while the new version confidently delivers wrong answers that vary each attempt
ChatGPT answers the same questions perfectly when used directly, but Siri mangles the answers even when using ChatGPT as its backend — suggesting the integration itself is broken
OpenAI may be getting negative brand equity from the Apple partnership, since Siri's attribution makes ChatGPT look responsible for wrong answers it wouldn't give on its own
Google's AI Overview performed nearly as badly as Siri, at one point citing a South Dakota school as the North Dakota champion
money_quotes: If Siri were a quarterback, it would be drummed out of the NFL.
It's like Siri is a special-ed student permitted to take an exam with the help of a tutor who knows the correct answers, and still flunks.
Siri with Apple Intelligence, with ChatGPT integration enabled, is a massive regression.
New Siri — powered by Apple Intelligence™ with ChatGPT integration enabled — gets the answer completely but plausibly wrong, which is the worst way to get it wrong.
I feel confident predicting that Lower Brule will never win the North Dakota state championship ... because Lower Brule is a school in South Dakota.
Not only is OpenAI getting no money from Apple out of this arrangement, but that the net brand equity they're getting from it might be negative.
As it stands, Apple is getting a scapegoat more than a partner out of this deal.
Perhaps Apple should partner with Google to add Gemini as another 'world knowledge' partner to power — or is it weaken? — Apple Intelligence.
key_insight: Apple Intelligence has made Siri worse, not better — turning it from a humble assistant that knew its limits into a confidently wrong one that bungles answers even with ChatGPT's help.
